title: Dijkstra (Shortest Paths)
code: static int NetworkDelayTime(int[][] times, int n, int k)
while (pq.TryDequeue(out int u, out int d))

frames.caption: Init: dist[0]=0, all others ∞. Push the source (0, 0) onto the min-PQ.
frames.note: Dijkstra starts every vertex at distance infinity except the source at 0, then drives everything off a min-priority-queue keyed by distance. The heap is what makes the greedy choice cheap: it always hands back the closest unsettled vertex in O(log V). With non-negative weights, that closest-first order means each vertex is finalized the first time it leaves the heap.

frames.caption: Pop the closest unsettled node: (0, 0). dist[0]=0 is now FINAL — settle 0.
frames.note: Dijkstra is BFS for weighted graphs: instead of a FIFO queue it pops from a MIN-HEAP keyed by best-known distance, so the next vertex pulled is always the closest unsettled one. The moment a vertex is popped at its recorded distance, that distance is final — no later path can beat it, because every remaining edge has non-negative weight and every other frontier vertex is already at least as far. That greedy "closest first" order is the whole algorithm.

frames.caption: Relax 0->1 (w=4): 0+4=4 < ∞ → set dist[1]=4, push (1, 4).
frames.note: Relax edge u->v: if dist[u] + w < dist[v] we found a cheaper route to v, so we lower dist[v] and push (v, new dist) onto the heap. .NET's PriorityQueue has no decrease-key, so we just push a duplicate and let the smaller one surface first. This is exactly where non-negativity matters: a negative w could make an already-settled vertex improvable later, which would break the "settled is final" guarantee — that case needs Bellman-Ford instead.

frames.caption: Relax 0->2 (w=1): 0+1=1 < ∞ → set dist[2]=1, push (2, 1).
frames.note: Relax edge u->v: if dist[u] + w < dist[v] we found a cheaper route to v, so we lower dist[v] and push (v, new dist) onto the heap. .NET's PriorityQueue has no decrease-key, so we just push a duplicate and let the smaller one surface first. This is exactly where non-negativity matters: a negative w could make an already-settled vertex improvable later, which would break the "settled is final" guarantee — that case needs Bellman-Ford instead.

frames.caption: All out-edges of 0 relaxed — 0 is settled. dist[0]=0 stands.
frames.note: Every out-edge of u has been relaxed, so u is fully SETTLED: dist[u] is its true shortest distance from the source and we will never touch it again. We color the tree edge we arrived on as the shortest path. Because each vertex settles once and each edge is relaxed once, the cost is O(E log V) — a log factor over BFS, the price of the heap.

frames.caption: Pop the closest unsettled node: (2, 1). dist[2]=1 is now FINAL — settle 2.
frames.note: Dijkstra is BFS for weighted graphs: instead of a FIFO queue it pops from a MIN-HEAP keyed by best-known distance, so the next vertex pulled is always the closest unsettled one. The moment a vertex is popped at its recorded distance, that distance is final — no later path can beat it, because every remaining edge has non-negative weight and every other frontier vertex is already at least as far. That greedy "closest first" order is the whole algorithm.

frames.caption: Relax 2->1 (w=2): 1+2=3 < 4 → set dist[1]=3, push (1, 3).
frames.note: Relax edge u->v: if dist[u] + w < dist[v] we found a cheaper route to v, so we lower dist[v] and push (v, new dist) onto the heap. .NET's PriorityQueue has no decrease-key, so we just push a duplicate and let the smaller one surface first. This is exactly where non-negativity matters: a negative w could make an already-settled vertex improvable later, which would break the "settled is final" guarantee — that case needs Bellman-Ford instead.

frames.caption: Relax 2->3 (w=5): 1+5=6 < ∞ → set dist[3]=6, push (3, 6).
frames.note: Relax edge u->v: if dist[u] + w < dist[v] we found a cheaper route to v, so we lower dist[v] and push (v, new dist) onto the heap. .NET's PriorityQueue has no decrease-key, so we just push a duplicate and let the smaller one surface first. This is exactly where non-negativity matters: a negative w could make an already-settled vertex improvable later, which would break the "settled is final" guarantee — that case needs Bellman-Ford instead.

frames.caption: All out-edges of 2 relaxed — 2 is settled. dist[2]=1 stands.
frames.note: Every out-edge of u has been relaxed, so u is fully SETTLED: dist[u] is its true shortest distance from the source and we will never touch it again. We color the tree edge we arrived on as the shortest path. Because each vertex settles once and each edge is relaxed once, the cost is O(E log V) — a log factor over BFS, the price of the heap.

frames.caption: Pop the closest unsettled node: (1, 3). dist[1]=3 is now FINAL — settle 1.
frames.note: Dijkstra is BFS for weighted graphs: instead of a FIFO queue it pops from a MIN-HEAP keyed by best-known distance, so the next vertex pulled is always the closest unsettled one. The moment a vertex is popped at its recorded distance, that distance is final — no later path can beat it, because every remaining edge has non-negative weight and every other frontier vertex is already at least as far. That greedy "closest first" order is the whole algorithm.

frames.caption: Relax 1->3 (w=1): 3+1=4 < 6 → set dist[3]=4, push (3, 4).
frames.note: Relax edge u->v: if dist[u] + w < dist[v] we found a cheaper route to v, so we lower dist[v] and push (v, new dist) onto the heap. .NET's PriorityQueue has no decrease-key, so we just push a duplicate and let the smaller one surface first. This is exactly where non-negativity matters: a negative w could make an already-settled vertex improvable later, which would break the "settled is final" guarantee — that case needs Bellman-Ford instead.

frames.caption: All out-edges of 1 relaxed — 1 is settled. dist[1]=3 stands.
frames.note: Every out-edge of u has been relaxed, so u is fully SETTLED: dist[u] is its true shortest distance from the source and we will never touch it again. We color the tree edge we arrived on as the shortest path. Because each vertex settles once and each edge is relaxed once, the cost is O(E log V) — a log factor over BFS, the price of the heap.

frames.caption: Pop (1, 4) — but 4 > dist[1]=3: STALE, skip it.
frames.note: This heap entry is STALE: the distance we popped is larger than dist[u], meaning u was already settled through a cheaper route while this duplicate sat in the heap. We skip it in O(1) and move on. Pushing duplicates and skipping stale pops is the standard way to run Dijkstra with a heap that lacks decrease-key.

frames.caption: Pop the closest unsettled node: (3, 4). dist[3]=4 is now FINAL — settle 3.
frames.note: Dijkstra is BFS for weighted graphs: instead of a FIFO queue it pops from a MIN-HEAP keyed by best-known distance, so the next vertex pulled is always the closest unsettled one. The moment a vertex is popped at its recorded distance, that distance is final — no later path can beat it, because every remaining edge has non-negative weight and every other frontier vertex is already at least as far. That greedy "closest first" order is the whole algorithm.

frames.caption: All out-edges of 3 relaxed — 3 is settled. dist[3]=4 stands.
frames.note: Every out-edge of u has been relaxed, so u is fully SETTLED: dist[u] is its true shortest distance from the source and we will never touch it again. We color the tree edge we arrived on as the shortest path. Because each vertex settles once and each edge is relaxed once, the cost is O(E log V) — a log factor over BFS, the price of the heap.

frames.caption: Pop (3, 6) — but 6 > dist[3]=4: STALE, skip it.
frames.note: This heap entry is STALE: the distance we popped is larger than dist[u], meaning u was already settled through a cheaper route while this duplicate sat in the heap. We skip it in O(1) and move on. Pushing duplicates and skipping stale pops is the standard way to run Dijkstra with a heap that lacks decrease-key.

frames.caption: PQ empty — shortest distances from 0 are [0, 3, 1, 4]. Network delay = max = 4.
frames.note: Heap drained, every vertex settled: dist holds the true shortest distance from the source to each node, and the highlighted edges form the shortest-path tree. For LC 743 the network delay is the max of these (the signal reaches the farthest node), or -1 if any node stayed ∞. Total cost O(E log V), the standard tool for single-source shortest paths on non-negative weights.
